papers.title: Prices vs. Quantities: Robust Regulation
papers.abstract: This paper revisits the classic instrument choice problem in a setting with consumption externalities, through the lens of robust mechanism design. A regulator can implement any incentive-compatible policy but is uncertain about how individual demand is correlated with marginal externalities, and evaluates policies by worst-case welfare. The optimal policy is a quantity control: a floor for positive externalities and a ceiling for negative externalities. If the sign of the correlation is known, a uniform tax or subsidy can be optimal. The framework also applies to regulatory uncertainty and costly screening, providing a welfare-based explanation for the prevalence of non-price policies.
papers.summary: This paper examines the classic prices vs. quantities instrument choice problem using robust mechanism design, where a regulator facing uncertainty about the correlation between individual demand and marginal externalities chooses policies based on worst-case welfare outcomes. The methodology is theoretical, employing robust optimization techniques within a mechanism design framework. The key finding is that quantity controls (floors for positive externalities, ceilings for negative externalities) are optimal under uncertainty, while uniform taxes/subsidies can be optimal only when the sign of the demand-externality correlation is known, providing a welfare-theoretic rationale for the widespread use of non-price regulatory instruments.

papers.title: The Suicide Region: Option Games and the Race to Artificial General Intelligence
papers.abstract: Standard real options theory predicts delay in exercising the option to invest or deploy when extreme asset volatility or technological uncertainty are present. However, in the current race to develop artificial general intelligence (AGI), sovereign actors are exhibiting behaviors contrary to theoretical predictions: the US and China are accelerating AI investment despite acknowledging the potential for catastrophic failure from AGI misalignment. We resolve this puzzle by formalizing the AGI race as a continuous-time preemption game with endogenous existential risk. In our model, the cost of failure is no longer bounded only by the sunk cost of investment (I), but rather a systemic ruin parameter (D) that is correlated with development velocity and shared globally. As the disutility of catastrophe is embedded in both players' payoffs, the risk term mathematically cancels out of the equilibrium indifference condition. This creates a "suicide region" in the investment space where competitive pressures force rational agents to deploy AGI systems early, despite a negative risk-adjusted net present value. Furthermore, we show that "warning shots" (sub-existential disasters) will fail to deter AGI acceleration, as the winner-takes-all nature of the race remains intact. The race can only be halted if the cost of ruin is internalized, making safety research a prerequisite for economic viability. We derive the critical private liability threshold required to restore the option value of waiting and propose mechanism design interventions that can better ensure safe AGI research and socially responsible deployment.
papers.summary: This paper examines why the US and China are accelerating AI investment despite catastrophic AGI risks, contradicting standard real options theory that predicts investment delays under extreme uncertainty. Using a continuous-time preemption game with endogenous existential risk, the authors show that when catastrophic failure costs are shared globally, the risk term cancels out of players' equilibrium conditions, creating a "suicide region" where competitive pressures force rational agents to deploy AGI early despite negative risk-adjusted NPV. The key contribution is demonstrating that only internalizing ruin costs through private liability can restore proper investment incentives and halt the dangerous race dynamics.

papers.title: Existence of Equilibrium Mechanisms in Generalized Principal-Agent Problems with Interacting Teams
papers.abstract: We study incentive design when multiple principals simultaneously design mechanisms for their respective teams in environments with strategic spillovers. In this environment, each principal's set of incentive-compatible mechanisms--those that satisfy their own agents' incentive compatibility constraints--depends on the mechanisms offered by the other teams. Following a classic example by Myerson (1982), such games may lack equilibrium due to discontinuities in the correspondence of incentive-compatible mechanisms. We establish general conditions for equilibrium existence by introducing a novel approach that involves tracking both the outcome distributions along the truthful-obedient path and the sets of outcome distributions achievable through unilateral deviations, thereby providing a foundation for analyzing a wide range of multi-principal mechanism design with team production and agency problems.
papers.summary: This paper addresses the fundamental question of whether equilibria exist in multi-principal mechanism design games where teams interact strategically and each principal's feasible incentive-compatible mechanisms depend on other principals' choices. The authors develop a novel theoretical approach that tracks both truthful equilibrium outcomes and deviations to handle discontinuities in the incentive-compatibility correspondence that can prevent equilibrium existence (as shown in Myerson 1982). The key contribution is establishing general sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence in these complex environments, providing theoretical foundations for analyzing strategic interactions in team production and multi-agency settings.

papers.title: A Real-Options-Aware Multi-Criteria Framework for Ex-Ante Real Estate Redevelopment Use Selection
papers.abstract: A growing share of the existing real estate stock exhibits persistent underperformance that can no longer be explained by cyclical market phases or inadequate maintenance alone. In many cases, technically recoverable assets located in non-marginal contexts fail to generate economic value consistent with the capital immobilized. This condition reflects a structural misalignment between intended use and effective demand rather than episodic market weakness, and calls for a decision framework capable of integrating value, risk, complexity, and irreversibility in strategic use selection. This study proposes a decision-analytic framework for the ex-ante selection of intended use in real estate redevelopment processes. The framework integrates real-options logic on irreversibility and managerial flexibility with a multi-criteria decision-analysis structure, enabling comparative evaluation of expected economic value, market and operational risk, technical and managerial complexity, and time-to-income. By treating redevelopment primarily as a problem of strategic option selection rather than design or financial optimization, the framework operationalizes option value preservation through disciplined ex-ante screening. Illustrative cases demonstrate how this integration of real options reasoning and MCDA reduces over-complexification and misalignment across different asset types and urban contexts.
papers.summary: This paper addresses how to select optimal intended uses for underperforming real estate assets requiring redevelopment by developing a decision framework that treats use selection as a strategic option choice rather than a design optimization problem. The methodology combines real options theory (incorporating irreversibility and managerial flexibility) with multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) to evaluate alternatives across dimensions of economic value, risk, complexity, and time-to-income. The key contribution is an integrated analytical framework that preserves option value through disciplined ex-ante screening, reducing misalignment between asset use and market demand while avoiding over-complexification in redevelopment decisions.

papers.title: Stochastic Optimization and Coupling
papers.abstract: We study optimization problems in which a linear functional is maximized over probability measures that are dominated by a given measure according to an integral stochastic order in an arbitrary dimension. We show that the following four properties are equivalent for any such order: (i) the test function cone is closed under pointwise minimum, (ii) the value function is affine, (iii) the solution correspondence has a convex graph with decomposable extreme points, and (iv) every ordered pair of measures admits an order-preserving coupling. As corollaries, we derive the extreme and exposed point properties involving integral stochastic orders such as multidimensional mean-preserving spreads and stochastic dominance. Applying these results, we generalize Blackwell's theorem by completely characterizing the comparisons of experiments that admit two equivalent descriptions -- through instrumental values and through information technologies. We also show that these results immediately yield new insights into information design, mechanism design, and decision theory.
papers.summary: This paper investigates stochastic optimization problems where a linear functional is maximized over probability measures constrained by integral stochastic orders in arbitrary dimensions. Using theoretical analysis, the authors establish the equivalence of four key properties: closure of test function cones under pointwise minimum, affinity of the value function, convexity properties of solution correspondences, and existence of order-preserving couplings between measure pairs. The main contribution is a unified framework that generalizes Blackwell's theorem on experiment comparison and provides new theoretical foundations for information design, mechanism design, and decision theory through the characterization of integral stochastic orders.

papers.title: Self-Confirming Mechanisms
papers.abstract: This paper studies mechanism design environments in which the designer does not know the distribution of agents' private information a priori and instead learns from agents' behavior induced by the mechanism itself. We formalize a notion of self-confirming mechanisms and a refinement thereof, capturing the idea that an equilibrium mechanism is optimal given the designer's belief and that this belief is consistent with the information produced by the mechanism. We establish a fictitious revelation principle, showing that any incentive-compatible mechanism can be represented as a direct mechanism with filtered type reports that preserve the original mechanism's informational content. Applying the framework to a monopoly problem, we show that, subject to an equilibrium refinement, dominant-strategy self-confirming mechanisms are exactly posted-price mechanisms with locally revenue-maximizing prices.
papers.summary: This paper examines mechanism design when the designer must learn agents' type distributions from the mechanism's own induced behavior rather than knowing them a priori. Using theoretical analysis, the authors develop a "fictitious revelation principle" demonstrating that any incentive-compatible mechanism can be represented as a direct mechanism with appropriately filtered type reports. The key contribution is proving that under equilibrium refinement, dominant-strategy self-confirming mechanisms in monopoly settings are equivalent to posted-price mechanisms with locally revenue-maximizing prices.

papers.title: Delegation in Strategic Environments and Equilibrium Uniqueness
papers.abstract: We ask when a normal-form game yields a single equilibrium prediction, even if players can coordinate by delegating play to an intermediary such as a platform or a cartel. Delegation outcomes are modeled via coarse correlated equilibria (CCE) when the intermediary cannot punish deviators, and via the set of individually rational correlated profiles (IRCP) when it can. We characterize games in which the IRCP or the CCE is unique, uncovering a structural link between these solution concepts. Our analysis also provides new conditions for the uniqueness of classical correlated and Nash equilibria that do not rely on the existence of dominant strategies. The resulting equilibria are robust to players' information about the environment, payoff perturbations, pre-play communication, equilibrium selection, and learning dynamics. We apply these results to collusion-proof mechanism design.
papers.summary: This paper investigates when games have unique equilibrium predictions when players can delegate decisions to intermediaries (platforms, cartels) that may or may not have punishment power. The authors use theoretical analysis to characterize games with unique coarse correlated equilibria (CCE) when intermediaries cannot punish deviators and unique individually rational correlated profiles (IRCP) when they can, establishing structural connections between these solution concepts. The key contribution is providing new conditions for equilibrium uniqueness that don't require dominant strategies, yielding robust predictions across various information structures, payoff perturbations, and learning dynamics, with applications to collusion-proof mechanism design.

papers.title: Revenue Non-monotonicity in Matching Markets
papers.abstract: The Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) mechanism is infamously revenue   non-monotone in combinatorial auctions. I.e., when a buyer increases   their value for a bundle of items, the total auction revenue may decrease.   Combinatorial auctions exhibit complementarities which broadly   result in complexities in auction theory. This brief note shows   that non-monotonicity in multi-item auctions is not a result of   complementarities, and in fact, VCG is revenue non-monotone even in matching   markets.
papers.summary: The paper investigates whether revenue non-monotonicity in the VCG mechanism stems from complementarities in multi-item auctions by examining matching markets where such complementarities are absent. Using theoretical analysis, the author demonstrates that VCG remains revenue non-monotone even in matching markets without complementarities. The key contribution is showing that revenue non-monotonicity is a fundamental property of VCG that persists beyond combinatorial auctions with complementarities.

papers.title: Robust Mechanism Design with Anonymous Information
papers.abstract: In practice, auction data are often endogenously censored and anonymous, revealing only limited outcome statistics rather than full bid profiles. We study robust auction design when the seller observes only aggregated, anonymous order statistics and seeks to maximize worst-case expected revenue over all product distributions consistent with the observed statistic. We show that simple and widely used mechanisms are robustly optimal. Specifically, posted pricing is robustly optimal given the distribution of the highest value; the Myerson auction designed for the unique consistent i.i.d. distribution is robustly optimal given the lowest value distribution; and the second-price auction with an optimal reserve is robustly optimal when an intermediate order statistic is observed and the implied i.i.d. distribution is regular above its reserve. More generally, for a broad class of monotone symmetric mechanisms depending only on the top k order statistics, including multi-unit and position auctions, the worst-case revenue is attained under the i.i.d. distribution consistent with the observed k-th order statistic. Our results provide a tractable foundation for non-discriminatory auction design, where fairness and privacy are intrinsic consequences of the information structure rather than imposed constraints.
papers.summary: This paper examines robust auction design when sellers only observe anonymous, aggregated bidding statistics rather than complete bid information, asking how to maximize worst-case expected revenue across all possible bidder value distributions consistent with these limited observations. Using theoretical mechanism design, the authors prove that simple, commonly-used mechanisms are robustly optimal: posted pricing when observing the highest value distribution, Myerson auctions when observing the lowest value distribution, and second-price auctions with reserves for intermediate order statistics. The key contribution demonstrates that for monotone symmetric mechanisms depending on top-k order statistics, worst-case revenue is achieved under i.i.d. distributions, providing theoretical justification for simple auction formats while naturally incorporating fairness and privacy through information constraints rather than explicit design requirements.

papers.title: One Model, Two Markets: Bid-Aware Generative Recommendation
papers.abstract: Generative Recommender Systems using semantic ids, such as TIGER (Rajput et al., 2023), have emerged as a widely adopted competitive paradigm in sequential recommendation. However, existing architectures are designed solely for semantic retrieval and do not address concerns such as monetization via ad revenue and incorporation of bids for commercial retrieval. We propose GEM-Rec, a unified framework that integrates commercial relevance and monetization objectives directly into the generative sequence. We introduce control tokens to decouple the decision of whether to show an ad from which item to show. This allows the model to learn valid placement patterns directly from interaction logs, which inherently reflect past successful ad placements. Complementing this, we devise a Bid-Aware Decoding mechanism that handles real-time pricing, injecting bids directly into the inference process to steer the generation toward high-value items. We prove that this approach guarantees allocation monotonicity, ensuring that higher bids weakly increase an ad's likelihood of being shown without requiring model retraining. Experiments demonstrate that GEM-Rec allows platforms to dynamically optimize for semantic relevance and platform revenue.
papers.summary: This paper addresses how to integrate commercial monetization objectives (ad placement and bidding) into generative recommender systems that currently only optimize for semantic relevance. The authors develop GEM-Rec, a theoretical framework using control tokens to decouple ad placement decisions from item selection and a Bid-Aware Decoding mechanism that incorporates real-time bid values during inference. The key contribution is proving allocation monotonicity (higher bids increase ad likelihood) while enabling platforms to dynamically balance semantic relevance and revenue optimization without model retraining.

papers.title: A mechanism design overview of Sedna
papers.abstract: Sedna is a coded multi-proposer consensus protocol in which a sender shards a transaction payload into rateless symbols and disseminates them across parallel proposer lanes, providing high throughput and ``until decode'' privacy. This paper studies a sharp incentive failure in such systems. A cartel of lane proposers can withhold the bundles addressed to its lanes, slowing the chain's symbol accumulation while privately pooling the missing symbols. Because finalized symbols become public, the cartel's multi-slot information lead is governed by a chain level delay event where the chain fails to accumulate the $κ$ bundles needed for decoding by the honest horizon $t^\star=\lceil κ/m\rceil$. We characterize the resulting delay probability with KL-type large deviation bounds and show a knife edge pathology when the slack $Δ=t^\star m-κ$ is zero such that withholding a single bundle suffices to push inclusion into the next slot with high probability.   We propose \textsf{PIVOT-$K$}, a Sedna native pivotal bundle bounty that concentrates rewards on the $κ$ bundles that actually trigger decoding, and we derive explicit incentive compatibility conditions against partial and coalition deviations. We further show that an adaptive sender ``ratchet'' that excludes lanes whose tickets were not redeemed collapses multi-slot withholding into a first slot deficit when $t^\star\ge 2$, reducing the required bounty by orders of magnitude. We close by bounding irreducible within slot decode races and providing parameter guidance and numerical illustrations. Our results show that for realistic parameters Sedna can reduce MEV costs to 0.04\% of the transaction value.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes the incentive design problem in Sedna, a multi-proposer blockchain consensus protocol where transaction payloads are sharded across parallel lanes for high throughput and privacy. Using mechanism design theory and large deviation analysis, the authors identify a critical vulnerability where cartels of lane proposers can profitably withhold transaction bundles to gain private information advantages, with the attack success probability exhibiting knife-edge behavior when the slack parameter Δ=0. The key contribution is proposing PIVOT-K, an incentive-compatible bounty mechanism that concentrates rewards on the κ bundles needed for decoding, combined with an adaptive "ratchet" system that reduces required bounties by orders of magnitude when the honest decoding horizon t*≥2.

papers.title: Learning, Misspecification, and Cognitive Arbitrage in Linear-Quadratic Network Games
papers.abstract: We study strategic interaction in linear-quadratic network games where agents act on subjective, misspecified models of their environment. Agents observe noisy aggregate signals generated by local network externalities and interpret them through simplified conjectures, such as constant or mean-field representations. We characterize the long-run behavior using the Berk-Nash equilibrium (BNE) concept, establishing conditions under which BNE diverges from the Nash equilibrium of the perfectly specified game. We quantify this divergence using a Value of Misspecification (VoM) metric. Building on this framework, we introduce "cognitive arbitrage" -- a design paradigm where a system designer strategically shapes agents' conjectures via minimal observation distortions to steer equilibrium outcomes. We formulate the cognitive arbitrage problem as a Stackelberg optimization with closed-form solutions and prove the convergence of a two-time-scale learning algorithm to the optimal BNE. Our results provide a principled framework for influencing behavior in networked systems with bounded rationality, offering a new perspective on mechanism design that operates on agents' representations rather than their incentives.
papers.summary: This paper examines how strategic agents in linear-quadratic network games behave when they hold misspecified beliefs about their environment, learning from noisy aggregate signals through simplified conjectures. Using theoretical analysis, the authors characterize long-run behavior via Berk-Nash equilibrium and establish conditions under which misspecification creates systematic deviations from standard Nash equilibrium, quantified through a Value of Misspecification metric. The key contribution is introducing "cognitive arbitrage" - a mechanism design approach where a system designer optimally distorts agents' observations to manipulate their beliefs and steer equilibrium outcomes, providing closed-form Stackelberg solutions and convergent learning algorithms for this new paradigm of influencing behavior through belief manipulation rather than direct incentive design.
